Query: Which crown would be suitable for a patient with high
caries index, minimum horizontal overlap of maxillary central incisors?

Options:
A: Metal ceramic crown
B: Three fourth crown
C: Porcelain jacket crown
D: Aluminium crown
Retrieved Documents:
Document [0] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Oversized Crown) To minimize the crown diameter, the metal edges of the best-sized crown can be given a V cut on the buccal surface. The cut edges are then reapproximated. The overlapping margins are then welded together. [6]
Document [1] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation) Operators can use the number 137 pliers can be used for circumferential contouring. Contouring pliers deliver a belling effect in the middle third of the crown. [19] Johnson contouring pliers with a No. 114 are used for cervical crown adaptation on the cervical third of the buccal and lingual surfaces. A curved beak plier No. 114 is then used to contour the buccal and lingual surfaces. Curved beak pliers may also contour the crown's proximal areas. [20] [13] Lastly, the crimping pliers are used to crimp the cervical margin 1 mm circumferentially for final adaptation and ensure that the crown margin's outline follows the gingival margin of the tooth. The operators can check the final adaptation of the crown by taking a radiograph to verify the gingival contour and extension, ensuring the complete coverage of the tooth. [13] [21]
Document [2] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Undersized Crown) The crown wall of the best-fitting crown can be cut buccally or lingually, and an additional piece of 0.004-inch orthodontic stainless steel band can be welded over the gap that is then contoured to the desired form.
Document [3] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown) The operator can select the crown using different methods: Measuring the mesiodistal dimension of the tooth before tooth preparation Trial and error method Crown selection after tooth preparation
Document [4] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance) Primary teeth are relatively at a larger risk of caries than permanent teeth. They also have larger pulp with prominent pulp horns, which places exact demands on cavity design. Hence, a full-coverage restoration is required. Although stainless steel crowns have an aesthetic disadvantage, they are still the treatment of choice for the following reasons: Durability Efficiency Longevity Cost-effectiveness Reliability Even though other tooth-colored restorations are available in the market, they are costly or require more tooth reduction, increasing the chances of pulp exposure. [15] [29]
Document [5] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Occlusal Reduction) Operators can use 69L or 169L bur to reduce 1.5 to 2.0 mm of the occlusal surface. The cusps original contour needs to be preserved. Occlusal depth guidance grooves could be given for better reduction. If a sufficient amount of occlusal surface has already been lost due to caries, marginal ridges of neighboring teeth can be used as a reference. [18]
Document [6] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Contraindications) Patients with severe caries with signs and symptoms of irreversible pulpitis or dental sepsis Crown was severely destroyed due to caries and is non-restorable Children at risk of bacterial endocarditis
Document [7] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation) Crown adaptation is critical for retention and gum protection. A poorly adapted crown is a source of plaque and bacterial accumulation, resulting in gingivitis and recurrent cervical caries. Spedding advocated 2 key principles to adjust a stainless steel crown correctly. The operator must determine the appropriate occlusal-gingival crown length. Crown margins should be contoured circumferentially to follow the natural contours of the marginal gingiva of the tooth. Steps
Document [8] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Open-faced Stainless Steel Crown) Stainless steel crowns can be rendered more aesthetically pleasing in anterior teeth by trimming the labial surface of the crown and making it open-facing. After fitting, A composite resin is placed into the crown's labial face. Aesthetic crowns with prefabricated tooth-colored buccal and occlusal facings are also available from specialized suppliers. [8]
Document [9] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- With Adjacent Stainless Steel Crowns) When more than 1 crown is required in a quadrant, both teeth should be prepared during the same visit. There should be slightly more than usual reduction on the adjacent proximal surfaces. This makes it easier to place multiple crowns. Before reducing another tooth, the occlusal reduction of one should be completed. Simultaneous reduction of both teeth results in improper reduction. There is a risk of mesiodistal space loss if there are proximal caries on adjacent teeth. To restore carious adjacent teeth with stainless steel crowns, the preparations should be modified to fit the teeth with smaller crowns than normal. Rather than more proximal reduction, further reduction of the buccal and lingual walls is carried out. [6] [10] [23]
Document [10] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Adaptation) Place the crown linguo-buccally on the prepared tooth, applying pressure in the buccal direction so the crown slips over the buccal surface and into the gingival sulcus. There is friction as the crown slides over the buccal bulge. Crown positioning be difficult if the crown is too small or if the buccal bulge is too excessive. Choose a larger crown or reduce the tooth's buccal bulge in this case. After placing the selected crown over the prepared tooth, ask the patient to bite over it. Check for preliminary occlusal and marginal ridge relation. Previously, scratch a mark on the stainless steel crown at the level of the free gingival margin. Scratch or dotted lines indicate the gingival contour and the portion of the crown to be removed. Remove the crown and trim or cut away the additional crown portion gingivally 1 mm below the scratch line with a crown and bridge scissors or, preferably, a large abrasive wheel (“heatless stone”) that allows for more precise reduction. Repeat the procedure until the adequate extension of 1 mm beneath the gingival margin is achieved.
Document [11] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction) Scientific evidence favors stainless steel crowns as restoration of choice in young children with high caries risk (see Image. Dental Caries). [4] Despite its many advantages, the use of stainless steel crowns is still unpopular among some professionals who see them as difficult to use, even though they are easier to place than intracoronal restorations and have much better long-term outcomes (see Images. Stainless Steel Crown, 1-Year Follow-Up, Stainless Steel Crown, 2-Year Follow-Up). [5]
Document [12] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Removal of Caries) Caries removal and pulpotomy procedures can be performed before or after crown preparation, although most crown preparation can be done while excavating caries. [6] [8] [15]
Document [13] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Crown Extension for Deep Subgingival Caries) Teeth with caries extending apically to the crown margin should have these areas filled with restoration before preparing the tooth to allow an appropriate finishing line for the crown, or a stainless steel extension could be soldered to the crown to form a flange for this area. [6]
Document [14] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure) Place orthodontic separators between the contact point of the primary molar for 4 or 5 days to create room for the crown. Select the smallest-sized crown covering all the cusps, approaching the contact points, and giving a 'spring back' effect. Adjustments of the crown are to be made using band-forming pliers if necessary. Cementation of the crown with glass ionomer cement. The tooth must be maintained in occlusion until the cement sets, followed by excess removal using floss. [25] [26] The Hall technique should be chosen as an alternative when the traditional approach with crown preparation is implausible. [25]
Document [15] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Buccal/Lingual Reduction) Reducing the buccal and lingual surfaces is the third step in the preparation. The reduction of the buccal and lingual mucosa is optional. Some authors suggested preparing the buccal and lingual walls to create a gingivally inclined long bevel to make crown placement easier. On the other hand, others advocated for none to minimal (0.5 to 1 mm) preparation on the vestibular and lingual aspect of the crown, unless pronounced enamel convexity should only be reduced by a small amount. [12] Duggal and Curzon recommended trying the selected crown before lingual and buccal reduction. [13] Lastly, the operator should round all line and point angles. Occlusal-lingual and occlusal-buccal angles are beveled at 45 degrees. Bur is held parallel to the tooth’s long axis, and one should carefully blend the surfaces. Remove any remaining caries and perform appropriate pulp therapy if pulp involvement is present. The rubber dam should be left in place until cementing the crown. If it interferes with the seating and adaptation of the crown, it can be removed after tooth preparation. [6]
Document [16] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation) A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is the preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]
Document [17] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Complications -- Marginal Discrepancy) Several researchers have extensively demonstrated the association between gingivitis and marginal defects, such as impaired marginal adaptation and insufficient removal of excess cement. Careful adaptation of the margins before fitting is required, and post-fitting gingivitis can be minimized by diligently polishing the crown margin. A preventive regimen, including oral hygiene training, should be included in the treatment plan. [8] [27]

Document [22] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Introduction) Stainless steel crowns are the most commonly used restorative option for repairing and preserving the remaining tissue of severely damaged and decayed teeth (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown). They were introduced into pediatric dentistry in 1947, first described by Engel, and then popularised by Humphrey in 1950. Stainless steel crowns have outperformed other materials, such as amalgam and composite, in terms of durability and longevity for more than a half-century. In fact, no restorative material has provided the benefits of low cost, reliability, and durability when interim full-coronal coverage is required. [3] [4]

Document [24] (Title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Treatment / Management) Indirect cuspal coverage in the form of a crown or onlay may also function extremely well as an external splint, prohibiting the flexure of the crack. Guthrie et al observed that most cracks extended into the cervical third of the crown. In their study, they concluded that 25 of the 28 tested cracked teeth became asymptomatic after receiving a full-coverage crown. [30] A full-coverage crown allows for a more equal distribution of occlusal forces across the affected tooth due to the resistant form of the crown. This prevents the crack's flexure and inhibits it from propagating further and causing more damage. [30] However, tooth preparation for full-coverage crowns is invasive and increases the risk of devitalization. [31]

Document [27] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Finishing) A large green stone is used to create a knife-edge finish at the cervical margin of the crown. Document [28] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown) Loss of mesiodistal dimension resulting from space loss distal to the mandibular primary first molar complicates selecting an appropriate size crown (see Image. [13] Other factors should be considered when selecting the crown: occlusal anatomy, primate spaces, and crown height. [6]
Document [29] (Title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Clinical Significance) GICs are highly successful as luting agents for cementation of stainless steel crowns for deciduous teeth, cast metal crowns, fixed dental prostheses for permanent teeth, space maintainers, orthodontic bands, and brackets. Resin-modified GIC is used for luting zirconia and alumina-based crowns. GICs can repair defects in tooth structure before crown preparation to stabilize the weakened portions of the tooth.
Document [30] (Title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Indications) Indications for the use of stainless steel crowns: Following pulp therapy Multisurface caries restorations Patients at high risk of caries Deciduous teeth with developmental defects, such as amelogenesis imperfecta, dentinogenesis imperfecta, enamel hypoplasia Teeth with extensive wear Where it is likely that the restoration may fail, eg, the proximal box formed is extended beyond the anatomic line angles. Fractured teeth Abutment for space maintainer [6] Patients who are unlikely to attend follow up appointments [7]

Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912) Pregnancy-Related Procedures is a broad category of obstetric interventions that encompasses specific techniques such as بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery, each classified as a child procedure within this group. Both بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery represent assisted vaginal delivery methods used to facilitate safe childbirth when spontaneous vaginal delivery is not optimal, highlighting the importance of tailored procedural options in maternal care.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1440) إدمان الهيروين يُصنَّف كنوع من الاعتماد على الأدوية، أي أن تعاطي الهيروين يُعد حالة إدمان تتضمن الاعتماد الجسدي والنفسي على المخدر.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2564) الفصادة الضوئية تُعرّف كعملية وريدية، وتُصنّف العمليات الوردية ضمن فصادة البلازما؛ وبالتالي فإن الفصادة الضوئية تُعد نوعًا من فصادة البلازما.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2453) The areola is identified as a breast part, representing a specific anatomical component of the breast.
